coredata.dc:title: Quantum dot-crosslinked light-guiding hydrogels for sensing folate receptor-overexpressed cancer cells
coredata.dc:creator: Zhang, Guosheng
Zhou, Sheng
Yan, Guoqing
Jiang, Tongtong
Wang, Zhexiang
Guan, Zhaoyuan
Chen, Kui
Cao, Zhigang
Yu, Benli
coredata.dc:description: In this study, we report quantum dots (QDs)-crosslinked light-guiding hydrogels, which are used to detect folate receptor (FR)-overexpressed cancer cells. The QDs are modified by folic acid (FA). In order to avoid fluorescence quenching, the covalent bond is used to cross-link FA modified QDs (FA-QDs) with hydrogels. The fluorescence stability of the prepared FA-QDs-doped light-guiding hydrogels (FQLH) is outstanding. Cancer cells with overexpression of FR (breast cancer cells, MCF-7; cervical cancer cells, HeLa), cancer cells without expression of FR (lung cancer cells, A549), and normal cells (mouse embryonic fibroblast, 3T3) are used to test the sensing characteristics of FQLH. After FQLH is inserted into the MCF-7 solution, the fluorescence intensity increases linearly with time, and the result of the HeLa solution is similar. But there is no similar response into the A549 solution or 3T3 solution, which shows that FQLH can effectively and specifically detect FR-overexpressed cancer cells. Simultaneously, the MCF-7 as an example is studied in detail. The results show that the concentration of MCF-7 can be measured based on the rate of increased fluorescence intensity. The minimum detection concentration is 1.0 × 104/mL. Moreover, detection of other cancer cells can be achieved by doping the hydrogels with the corresponding targeted modified QDs. In addition, the prepared FQLH has excellent mechanical and light-guiding performance (loss is less than 0.0165 dB/mm), and it is promised to be implanted into the human body to achieve real-time detection in vivo，which shows a great potential in cancer detection and therapy.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Optical fiber biosensor
Receptor-overexpressed cancer cells detection
Quantum dots
Light-guiding hydrogels

Optical setup for sensing of sample cells
Mechanical and optical properties of FQLH
The observation of cells entering the FQLH
The principle and results for FR-overexpressed cancer cells sensing based on FQLH

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic illustration of FR-overexpressed cancer cells optical-sensing detection with the FA-QDs-doped-light-guiding hydrogels.
optical fiber biosensing system for cancer cells based on FQLH.

Characteristics of FA-QDs. (a) Schematic illustration of the synthesis of FA-QDs. (b) Variation of electrical conductivity. (c) TEM image of FA-QDs. Inset: AFM. (d) FTIR spectra. (e) Photoluminescence spectra of QDs and FA-QDs. Inset: Photographs of FA-QDs dispersed in deionized water (under UV exposure and under visible).

Characteristics of FA-QDs-doped-PAM-co-PAA hydrogels. (a) Effects of different doping ways on fluorescence intensity of FA-QDs (1-by covalent bonding, 2-by physical embedding). (b) FTIR spectra. (c) The cross-section SEM image after lyophilization. (d) Tensile stress-strain properties. (e) Compression and stretching properties. (f) Absorption spectra. Inset: transparency.

Characteristics of FQLH. (a) Mechanical flexibility. (b) Photos showed 632 nm laser light coupling to the FQLH. Top, without light coupling; bottom, light coupling. (c) Image showing LED light (475 nm) coupling to the FQLH through an MMF. Top, LED off; bottom, LED on. (d) The excitation spectrum of FQLH. (e) Optical attenuation of FQLH over the propagation length.

(a) The reversed microscope image of the MCF-7 cultured with PBS solution with time. (b) The reversed microscope image of the MCF-7 cocultured with FA-QDs-doped hydrogels with time. (I) immediately. (II) 1 min later. (III) 2 min later.

(a) The response of FQLH's luminescence spectra to MCF-7. (b) Fitting line between the fluorescence intensity and time. (c) Fluorescence spectra at various concertration of MCF-7. (d) The relationship between rate of increased fluorescence intensity and MCF-7 concentration. Inset: the relationship between fluorescence intensity in final status and MCF-7 concentration.

(a) The fluorescence intensity of the FQLH in 3T3 and the QDs-doped-light-guiding hydrogels in MCF-7 with the time. (b) The fluorescence intensity of the FQLH in HeLa and A549. (c) The long-term luminescence intensity stability of FQLH. (d) The stability of the FQLH's fluorescence intensity in PBS solution at different temperatures. Inset: swell ratio of the FQLH at different temperatures. (e) The response of the FQLH's fluorescence intensity to temperature (f) The stability of the FQLH's fluorescence intensity in different concentrations of PBS solution. Inset: the swell ratio of the FQLH at different concentrations.
Confocal images of FQLH after inserting with MCF-7, HeLa, A549 and 3T3, respectively.

head.ce:title: Quantum dot-crosslinked light-guiding hydrogels for sensing folate receptor-overexpressed cancer cells

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Information Materials and Intelligent Sensing Laboratory of Anhui Province, Anhui University

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this study, we report quantum dots (QDs)-crosslinked light-guiding hydrogels, which are used to detect folate receptor (FR)-overexpressed cancer cells. The QDs are modified by folic acid (FA). In order to avoid fluorescence quenching, the covalent bond is used to cross-link FA modified QDs (FA-QDs) with hydrogels. The fluorescence stability of the prepared FA-QDs-doped light-guiding hydrogels (FQLH) is outstanding. Cancer cells with overexpression of FR (breast cancer cells, MCF-7; cervical cancer cells, HeLa), cancer cells without expression of FR (lung cancer cells, A549), and normal cells (mouse embryonic fibroblast, 3T3) are used to test the sensing characteristics of FQLH. After FQLH is inserted into the MCF-7 solution, the fluorescence intensity increases linearly with time, and the result of the HeLa solution is similar. But there is no similar response into the A549 solution or 3T3 solution, which shows that FQLH can effectively and specifically detect FR-overexpressed cancer cells. Simultaneously, the MCF-7 as an example is studied in detail. The results show that the concentration of MCF-7 can be measured based on the rate of increased fluorescence intensity. The minimum detection concentration is 1.0 × 104/mL. Moreover, detection of other cancer cells can be achieved by doping the hydrogels with the corresponding targeted modified QDs. In addition, the prepared FQLH has excellent mechanical and light-guiding performance (loss is less than 0.0165 dB/mm), and it is promised to be implanted into the human body to achieve real-time detection in vivo，which shows a great potential in cancer detection and therapy.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data underlying the results presented in this paper are not publicly available at this time but may be obtained from the authors upon reasonable request.

ce:section.ce:para: As a disease with a high risk of occurrence and mortality, cancer is a major threat to human health [1–4]. How to diagnose and prevent cancer at an early stage has become a primary global concern. Surgery is still the first line of defense against cancer treatment, but surgeons cannot guarantee that all malignant tissue is discovered and removed [5]. As a result, there should be an urgent need for a real-time, effective, and highly sensitive method for detecting cancer cells. Traditional cancer cells detection methods based on tumor markers, such as immunosensors and immunoassays, cannot meet the needs of clinical application [6,7] because these detection methods are incapable of determining whether cancer cells exist in human blood samples in a timely and intuitive manner [8]. Fluorescence imaging has been widely used in the clinic as another effective means of a cancer diagnosis. However, the detection depth (1/e attenuation length) of visible and near-infrared radiation in the organism is only about 10 mm due to the large scattering and absorption loss, and real-time detection in vivo remains challenging [9,10]. Simultaneously, the detection sensitivity is easily disrupted by the external environment.

Optical fiber sensing technology has shown considerable promise in the field of medical diagnosis in recent years, owing to its high sensitivity [11–13]. The use of QDs-doped biocompatible hydrogels to substitute traditional optical fibers for biosensing provides a possibility for rapid and convenient detection of cancer cells. However, the majority of the QDs-doped hydrogels currently reported are primarily used for detecting heavy metals, such as Pb2+ [14], Fe3+ [15], Cu2+ [16], Ag+ [17], and Hg2+ [18], as well as other chemical parameters including influenza virus (H5N1) [19], nitrite (NO2 −) [20], organophosphate pesticides (OPs) [21], and glucose [22].

Here, we present light-guiding hydrogels doped with FA modified QDs that are suitable for rapid and specific detection of FR-overexpressed cancer cells, and new ideas for the detection of different types of cancer cells and dynamic real-time in situ detection ( Fig. 1). The FR-overexpressed MCF-7 is used as an example to study the performance of the FQLH in detail. The currently reported methods for detecting MCF-7 include: Yang et al. report a carbon nanosphere (CNS)-based fluorescence “turn off/on” aptasensor, which is developed for targeted detection of MCF-7 [23]. Bae et al. present an approach for detecting the various breast cancer cells with the antibody-conjugated PFC/QD nanoemulsions, which is a type of bimodal imaging nanoprobe with unique MR and optical imaging capabilities [24]. However, fluorescence imaging technology is needed in the above methods, which increases the complexity of the operation. The proposed FQLH sensor can realize real-time detection and has a simple structure and convenient operation. After FQLH are inserted into the MCF-7 solution, the fluorescence intensity of the FQLH increases linearly. Simultaneously, the rate of increased fluorescence intensity of FQLH has a good linear correlation with different MCF-7 concentrations. As a result, we can detect the MCF-7 using the fluorescence intensity response to MCF-7. The minimum cell detection concentration is 1.0 × 104/mL. Moreover, HeLa with FR overexpression, A549 without FR expression, and normal cells-3T3 are used to study the specificity of FQLH for cancer cell recognition. The result of FQLH testing HeLa is similar to MCF-7. However, there is no linear response of FQLH in the A549 or 3T3 solution. This indicates that FQLH can effectively and specifically detect FR-overexpressed cancer cells. In addition, by modifying QDs of different fluorescence wavelengths with different targeting ligands, a variety of cancer cells can be simultaneously detected. Furthermore, rather than physical embedding, the FA-QDs are covalently doped into hydrogels. It not only prevents FA-QDs from escaping from the hydrogels but also protects fluorescent properties. The prepared FQLH has excellent mechanical properties and an optical loss of less than 0.0165 dB/mm. Thus, it can meet the requirements for long-term implantation in the human body for real-time cancer screening. The above findings suggest that the novel FQLH has a high potential for cancer detection and diagnosis.

The preparation process of FA-QDs is straightforward; EDC, NHS, and FA were dissolved in a 2 mL PBS solution. After stirring for 12 h in the dark, the QDs were added, and stirring continued in the dark for 48 h. Finally, dialysis was performed in deionized water for three days. Because the surface of QDs requires grafting with acrylic acid (AA) monomer in the follow-up experiments, the amount of substance ratio of EDC, NHS, FA, and the -NH2 group added was 1.5:1.5:0.5:1.

The content of -NH2 group was tested by conductance titration. The excess hydrochloric acid standard solution was added to the QDs solution, and hydrochloric acid reacted with -NH2 group to form ammonium salt. And then, sodium hydroxide (NaOH) solution was used to the titrate. The -NH2 group content of the QDs was determined using the amount of NaOH solution with constant conductivity. Eq. (1) gives the calculation formula.
(1)
C =
(
V
2
−
V
1
) *
C
1
V
3
where C was the content of -NH2 group in QDs, C1 was the NaOH solution concentration, V1 was the volume of NaOH solution used to neutralize H+ completely, V2 was the volume of NaOH solution used to react with the ammonium salt, and the V3 was the volume of the QDs solution.

There were two ways, physical embedding, and covalent bonding, that doped FA-QDs into hydrogels. To form a covalent bond, it was necessary to modify the FA-QDs with AA monomers in the hydrogels and record it as FA-QDs-A. The FA-QDs without AA modification were recorded as FA-QDs-B. The method and principle of grafting AA on the surface of FA-QDs was the same as those of grafting FA. EDC, NHS, and AA were dissolved in 2 mL PBS solution and stirred for 12 h in a dark environment. After that, the FA-QDs were added, and stirring continued in the dark for 48 h. Finally, dialysis was performed in deionized water for three days. The amount of substance ratio of EDC, NHS, AA, and the FA-QDs groups added was 1.5:1.5:1:1. The steps for the complete production process of FA-QDs-crosslinked hydrogels were summarized as follows: First, 1.8 g AM, 1.0 mL AA, 1.0 mL FA-QDs-A/FA-QDs-B (0.5 mM), 0.02 g N,N′-methylenebis (acrylamide) (BIS) and 100 µL 2-hydroxy-2-methylpropiophenone were mixed in 10 mL deionized water. The above precursor solution was injected into a pre-prepared mold. After UV exposure for 1 min, the FA-QDs-doped PAM-co-PAA hydrogels were obtained.

The FQLH was manufactured in three steps ( Fig. 2). First, the precursor solution was injected into a PVC tube mold by a syringe. The inner diameter of the tube was 1 mm. A multimode optical fiber (MMF) was inserted into the precursor solution and aligned to the center of the mold for light coupling. Then, the photo-crosslinking was completed by ultraviolet irradiation for 1 min. The FQLH was obtained from the mold by pressure.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Study on the swell ratio of FQLH
ce:section.ce:para:
The swell ratio of FQLH in PBS (pH = 7.4）solution was measured by the gravimetric method [25]. In the study of the effect of temperature on the swell ratio, the samples of FQLH were swollen in PBS solution at different temperatures (25, 30, 34, 40 °C) for 48 h to reach equilibrium. In studying the influence of water concentration on the swell ratio, the FQLH samples were swollen in the PBS solutions with different concentrations (0.1, 0.2, 0.3, 0.4 mol/L) for 48 h to reach equilibrium. The equilibrium swell ratio (ESR) was calculated as Eq. (2):
(2)
E S R =
(
W
s
−
W
d
)
W
d
Where Ws and Wd meant the weight of swollen FQLH and dried FQLH, respectively. Five replicates were performed for each temperature and the average value was reported.

Briefly, four types of cells (MCF-7, HeLa, A549 and 3T3) were seeded at the density of 4 × 103 cells/flask in cell culture flasks respectively, and incubated for 24 h to allow cell attachment and proliferation in RPMI-1640 medium containing 10% fetal bovine serum, 2 mM glutamine, 1 mM sodium pyruvate, 50 mg mL−1 streptomycin and 50 U mL−1 penicillin at 37 °C under 5% CO2. Afterwards, the cells were digested by trypsin and suspended for further experiments.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optical setup for sensing of sample cells
ce:section.ce:para: The experimental setup for cells identification and concentration detection was shown in Fig. 3. A semiconductor light-emitting diode (LED, λLED = 475 nm) was used to excite the FQLH through a wavelength division multiplex (WDM-475 nm/630 nm). The FQLH was dipped in the cell containing solution, and the FA-QDs fluorescence in response to cancer cells was detected through the other corresponding WDM port. A spectrometer (Ocean optics, QE65 Pro) was used to measure the fluorescence, and the data were processed by a computer. The sensing system could monitor cancer cells in real-time.

First, the FQLH had placed in cells solution for 60 min. And then, the FQLH was washed three times by PBS solution. Finally, the image of FQLH attached to cells was obtained through fluorescence microscopy as observed under the 60× objective.

As a well-known tissue-specific biomarker, the FR was highly expressed in various tumor cells, while the expression level in normal cells was significantly low [26–28]. Therefore, the FA was selected to observe the cancer cells in this study. The CdSe/ZnS (core/shell structure) QDs with the amino-functional group (-NH2) on the surface were selected as fluorescent nanomaterials in this work. Because the -NH2 group on the surface of the QDs could form amide bonds (-CO-NH-) with the carboxyl groups (-COOH) in FA ( Fig. 4a). CdSe/ZnS QDs was ideal fluorescent sensing material with broad absorption spectrum advantages, narrow emission band, good optical stability, and adjustable emission peak compared with traditional dyes. The ZnS shell could ensure high fluorescence stability and hypotoxicity. The content of -NH2 group on the surface of QDs was obtained according to the variation of electrical conductivity (Fig. 4b). First, the excess hydrogen ions (H+) neutralize the NaOH solution. The decrease in the H+ concentration leads to a rapid decrease in conductivity. The minimized solution conductivity was obtained when the excess H+ completely neutralized the NaOH solution. The conductivity of the solution remained almost unchanged when the ammonium salt formed on the surface of the QDs reacted with the NaOH solution. After the ammonium salt completely reacted with NaOH solution, the conductivity increased with the continuous addition of NaOH solution. The calculated content of -NH2 group was 30 mM. The transmission electron microscopy (TEM) was used to observe the FA-QDs, which confirmed that the size of FA-QDs was nanoscale, with an average diameter of 7.5 nm (Fig. 4c). The AFM was also used to verify the size of FA-QDs. The heights of the FA-QDs were 7.47 nm, which was in agreement with the result obtained in the TEM studies. To verify the formation of covalent bonds, the surface functional groups of the FA-QDs were characterized by the Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR) spectra (Fig. 4d). Compared with QDs, there was an obvious signal at 1632 cm−1 assigned to CO, which confirmed the existence of the -CO-NH- group in the FA-QDs and verified the formation of the FA-QDs [29]. To further investigate the fluorescence properties of FA-QDs, photoluminescence (PL) spectra of QDs and FA-QDs were tested on equal concentrations (Fig. 4e). Because the modification of FA increased the non-radiative recombination of FA-QDs, the fluorescence intensity of the FA-QDs decreased slightly, and the fluorescence peaks shifted red. The bright red fluorescence was observed when the FA-QDs solution was excited by UV light, while the solution was transparent under ambient light.The influence of modification on fluorescence characteristics was minimal and can be ignored. As a result, the QDs modified with FA were uniformly dispersed and exhibited good fluorescence stability, making them appropriate for use as an optical-sensing material.

A flexible, low loss and excellent mechanical FA-QDs-doped hydrogels were prepared by photo-crosslinking with polyacrylamide-co-polyacrylic acid (PAM-co-PAA) and FA-QDs. The fluorescence intensity of the hydrogels prepared via physical embedding decreased obviously with time, while the FA-QDs-A via covalent bonding remained almost unchanged ( Fig. 5a). The reason for this was that the modification made the surface passivated, which protected fluorescent properties. In addition, by incorporating complementary reactive functional groups, the coupling was more robust and avoided the diffusion of FA-QDs from hydrogels. The FTIR spectra were employed to demonstrate that the FA-QDs were cross-linked to the hydrogels (Fig. 5b). The broad absorption band was confirmed at 3400 cm−1 due to -OH stretching vibration [30]. The strong peaks at 1649 cm−1 and 1580 cm−1 were attributed to the CO and N–H stretching vibrations, respectively [31]. It indicated that FA-QDs were coupled to the hydrogels through a covalent bond. Another characteristic peak at 1414 cm−1 was C–H vibrations, while peak positions at 1269 cm−1 were due to C–N bond stretching [32]. Additionally, the significant peak at 1074 cm−1 could be assigned to C–O stretching vibration, which was the consequence of FA-doped PAM-co-PAA hydrogels cross-linking [33]. The cross-section SEM image of FA-doped hydrogels after lyophilization was observed (Fig. 5c). There are apparent pores within the polymer matrix. The pore size of hydrogels is approximately 100 µm, which allows small molecules and cells to pass through. The photocrosslinked PAM-co-PAA hydrogels exhibits excellent mechanical properties (Fig. 5d). Elongation up to 1650% at a tensile strength of 108.9 KPa was obtained with the FA-QDs-doped PAM-co-PAA hydrogels. The value was more than 13.8 and 2.4 times that of pure PAA (120%/50.4 KPa) and PAM (687%/87.3 KPa) hydrogels. The synthesized FA-QDs-doped hydrogels rapidly returned to its original shape immediately after removing finger pressure and could be easily stretched to 1650% strain, with no visible crack or breakage, which showed that FA-QDs-doped hydrogels have excellent mechanical properties (Fig. 5e). Moreover, the FA-QDs-doped hydrogels showed a low absorption and high transparency (through a 10 mm-length cube) in the visible range (Fig. 5f), making the multi-parameter fluorescence sensing possible.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Mechanical and optical properties of FQLH
ce:section.ce:para:
Soft hydrogels were more compatible with the biological system than traditional optical fibers. The prepared FQLH exhibited excellent mechanical flexibility which could easily be twisted around a rubber tube and bent ( Fig. 6a). The light-guiding properties of the fabricated FQLH were tested by coupling it to a laser. Light transmission along the FQLH exhibited good waveguide properties (Fig. 6b). The excitation light of 475 nm was then coupled to the FQLH via the MMF, and strong fluorescence was observed (Fig. 6c). The FQLH fluorescence peak (
λ = 631
nm
) was detected by the spectrometer (Fig. 6d). The excitation source was responsible for the peak at 475 nm. Furthermore, light intensity within the FQLH decreased linearly with propagation length, with an attenuation of 0.0165 dB/mm (Fig. 6e). Low light loss may be appealing for low-intensity and long-distance detection.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The observation of cells entering the FQLH
ce:section.ce:para: The inverted microscope was used to observe the condition of MCF-7 entering the FA-QDs-doped hydrogels ( Fig. 7). The size of MCF-7 was about 10 µm. The MCF-7 were cultured with PBS solution as a blank comparison (Fig. 7a). The microscope image did not significantly change with time. However, when the MCF-7 were cocultured with hydrogels solution, the gray shadows appeared in the image after 1 min, and a large area of gray shading appeared 2 min later (Fig. 7b). When the cells entered the hydorgels, the inverted microscope was unable to focus due to the position of the inverted microscope on the surface of the hydrogels, resulting in shadow imaging. This was an indication that a large number of cells had entered the FA-QDs-doped hydrogels.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The principle and results for FR-overexpressed cancer cells sensing based on FQLH
ce:section.ce:para: QDs photoluminescence (PL) occurs when the excited electron relaxes to the ground state and recombines with the hole [34]. Meanwhile, it was well-known that the increase of surface states or defects would cause the QDs PL to decrease due to Auger recombination [35]. During conjugation with FA on the surface of the QDs，which caused the defect state was produced and led to the occurrence of QDs fluorescence quenching [36]. Due to FR-overexpressed cancer cells combining with the FA-QDs doped in the hydrogels would decrease the surface defect state and increase the fluorescence intensity of the FA-QDs. According to this principle, the FQLH could be used to identify FR-overexpressed cancer cells specifically.

We started the test three minutes after FQLH was put into the solution, based on the time required for the cells to enter the hydrogels. MCF-7 dispersed in PBS solution often adhered to the wall after several hours, while FQLH could get the response signal after 3 min in MCF-7 solution. Therefore, the effect of adhesion was negligible. The response of FQLH's luminescence spectra to MCF-7 were measured with time ( Fig. 8a). The intensity of the fluorescence peak progressively grew over time, as indicated in the figure. The relationship between fluorescence intensity and time in MCF-7 was investigated (Fig. 8b). The fluorescence intensity was initially detected to increase and then remained unchanged with time. The enhancement of fluorescence intensity was caused by the interaction between MCF-7 and the specifically targeted modified FA-QDs. Finally, the fluorescence intensity remained intact because the interaction between MCF-7 and the FA-QDs in the hydrogels reached a stable level. The intensity of FQLH's fluorescence intensity has a strong linear relationship with time. The correlation coefficient square (R2) was 0.984. At the same time, we observed the fluorescence spectra at various cell concertration (Fig. 8c). The fluorescence intensity of the FQLH at different concentrations increased with time and showed a good linear relationship. As the MCF-7 concentration increases, the R2 is 0.997, 0.980, 0.984, 0.986, and 0.988, respectively. The rate of increased fluorescence intensity was faster when the concentration of cells was higher, and the fluorescence intensity was higher in final status. Combined with the influence of environmental fluctuations, the lowest concentration detected by this sensor was 1.0 × 104/mL. We also studied the relationship between the rate of increased fluorescence intensity of FQLH with different concentrations of MCF-7 (Fig. 8d). The inset shows the linear fitting results of the final fluorescence intensity of FQLH in different MCF-7 concentrations. Their linearity was excellent, and R2 is 0.948 and 0.9476, respectively. The FQLH was disposable because after sensing, it was difficult to completely remove MCF-7 from FQLH. Therefore, we had prepared multiple sets of FQLH for repeatability verification. By adjusting the excitation light power, ensure that the initial fluorescence intensity of the sensors was the same. The maximum deviation of the fluorescence intensity increase rate exhibited by the sensor was 3 /min, and the maximum deviation of the final fluorescence intensity was 23. As a result, we could not only detect specific cells, but also measure their concentration.

To show that FQLH can recognize MCF-7, it was placed in a solution of normal cells (3T3) and the fluorescence intensity was measured ( Fig. 9a). When the FQLH was placed in 3T3 solution, the fluorescence intensity fluctuation was very low with the time, in contrast to the MCF-7 results, showing that the FQLH had distinct recognition features. In addition, MCF-7 was detected using QD-doped hydrogels without FA modification. The fluorescence intensity of QD-doped hydrogels varied irregularly. The results proved the targeting effect of FA. Therefore, different targeted modifications of QDs could accurately identify different kinds of cells. Not only MCF-7 but also cervical, colon, ovarian and liver cancer cells also overexpressed FR [37]. To further improve the experiment, we had added the study about FR-overexpressed HeLa and A549 without FR expression [36]. When the FQLH was inserted into the HeLa solution, the fluorescence intensity showed a linear increase and then remained unchanged with time (Fig. 9b). The result was similar to MCF-7. However, in the A549 solution, the fluorescence intensity of the FQLH changed irregularly with time. It proved that the proposed FQLH could specifically recognize cancer cells with FR overexpression. To demonstrate the performance of the FQLH, the long-term stability of luminescence intensity was measured. The FQLH fluorescence intensity fluctuated slightly over 72 h at room temperature, but because the period necessary to detect cells was brief, the fluctuation could be overlooked (Fig. 9c). The fluctuations in temperature or water concentration would affect the swell ratio of the hydrogels, which might interfere with the signal of the FQLH. Therefore, the interference had been studied. When the temperature increased from 25 °C to 40 °C, the swell ratio of the FQLH only decreased 7.9% (Fig. 9d). The change of swell ratio caused by fluctuations in the range of 1 °C was only 0.53%. When the FQLH was placed in PBS solution at different temperatures, the fluorescence intensity could remain stable for a long time. Because temperature affected the fluorescence efficiency of FA-QDs, the initial fluorescence intensity of the FQLH was different. The temperature of the biological sample during the detection process was usually constant, the effect of temperature on the swelling rate could be ignored. Because temperature could affect the fluorescence efficiency of FA-QDs, the changes in the fluorescence intensity of FQLH at different temperatures had been discussed in more detail. During the heating process from 23 °C to 41 °C, the increased ratio of the FQLH's fluorescence intensity was −42.7619/°C (Fig. 9e). The linearity of the relationship with temperature was 98.31%. During the cooling process from 41 °C to 23 °C, the decrease ratio was −42.2738/°C, and the corresponding temperature response linearity was 97.85%. It could be seen that the change in fluorescence intensity caused by the fluctuation of 1 °C was very small; the extremely small temperature fluctuation would not affect the test result in the process of cancer cells detection. At the same time, the research results of PBS concentration interference were as follows: when the concentrations of PBS solution increased from 0.01 mol/L to 0.04 mol/L, the swell ratio of the FQLH increased 2.8% (Fig. 9f). The signal intensity of the sensor at different concentrations remained basically unchanged over time. The maximum relative value of intensity fluctuation was only 15. What’s more, the concentration of the solution in which the cells were stored was fixed and constant during the test to avoid cell death. Therefore, the fluctuations of solution concentration within a small range would not affect the test results. In a word, FQLH with excellent mechanical properties and low light loss was used as optical waveguides. It was a simple and effective sensing method to identify cancer cells and determine their concentration by detecting the fluorescence intensity of the targeted modified QDs in the hydrogels. Moreover, this provided a new idea for real-time in situ cell detection.

The confocal microscope was used to demonstrate that the FQLH could specifically recognize cancer cells with FR overexpression. As shown, many MCF-7 and HeLa could be observed on the surface of the FQLH, respectively ( Fig. 10). In contrast, only a tiny number of A549 and 3T3 could be seen on the surface of the FQLH. The FR was overexpressed in the MCF-7 and HeLa, which made cancer cells bind to the FA-QDs and firmly adhered to the FQLH. The red fluorescence was from the FA-QDs in FQLH.

We demonstrate a novel biosensor for FR-overexpressed cancer cells detection based on FA-QDs-doped light-guiding hydrogels. The MCF-7 is used as an example for FQLH sensing research. The minimum cell concentration detected of MCF-7 cells is 1.0 × 104/mL. By comparing the results of FQLH testing HeLa, A549, and 3T3, the specific recognition characteristics of the FQLH are proved. Through other targeted modifications to QDs, it is possible to recognize and explore the various cells in a broader sense. Furthermore, it has been demonstrated that doping QDs into hydrogels via covalent bonding is an effective strategy for preserving their fluorescence stability. The prepared FQLH has excellent mechanical properties and low optical loss. We anticipate that the FQLH sensor will open up new avenues for optical biosensing and will be widespread used in cancer cells detection.
The authors declare no conflicts of interest.

National Natural Science Foundation of China (61905001); National Key R&D Program of China (2016YFC0301900, 2016YFC0301901); Natural Science Foundation of Anhui Province (1908085QF276); Open Fund of Key Laboratory of Opto-Electronic Information Acquisition and Manipulation of Ministry of Education (OEIAM202006).

Guosheng Zhang: Investigation, Methodology, Writing – original draft, Writing – review & editing. Sheng Zhou: Conceptualization, Funding acquisition, Writing – review & editing. Guoqing Yan: Methodology, Writing – review & editing. Tongtong Jiang: Methodology, Investigation. Zhexiang Wang: Software, Validation. Zhaoyuan Guan: Software, Validation. Kui Chen: Validation. Zhigang Cao: Investigation. Benli Yu: Project administration.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Guosheng Zhang is currently a PhD student at Anhui University, China. He works on optical fiber sensors base on fluorescence nanomaterials, and their applications in chemical and biological detection.

Sheng Zhou received his Ph.D. degree in the Institute of Optics and Electronics, Chinese Academy of Sciences (CAS), China in 2017. Now, he has been an instructor at College of Physics and Material Science, Anhui University, China. His current research interests include flexible optical fiber devices for chemo- and biosensing purposes, and their engineering applications.
Guoqing Yan received his Ph.D. degree from Anhui University in China in 2017. His research focuses on design, construction and biological application of nano-scale functional optical devices.

Tongtong Jiang received his Ph.D. degree at University of Science and Technology of China in 2016. Her research focuses on the preparation of nanomaterials and their applications in photoelectric detection and flexible energy storage devices.
Zhexiang Wang is currently a Master student at Anhui University, China. He works on laser scanning confocal microscope for traced and recorded cellular uptake process.

Zhaoyuan Guan is currently a Master student at Anhui University, China. She works on design, preparation and performance study of multifunctional biomaterials with good biocompatibility, specific targeting and controllable drug release.

Kui Chen is currently a PhD student at University of Science and Technology of China, China. His research interest focuses on the synthesis and fabrication of wearable and stretchable energy storage materials.
Zhigang Cao received his Ph.D. at Anhui University in 2015. His current research interests include special fibers, fiber laser, fiber-optic sensors, and nonlinear fiber optics.

Benli Yu received his Ph.D. degree at University of Science and Technology of China in 1992. As a senior Professor of Anhui University, he is head of the Key Laboratory of Opto-Electronic Information Acquisition and Manipulation of Ministry of Education and became vice-president of Anhui University in 2014, and has more than 20 years experience in the applications of laser sensor techniques in the field of atmospheric environment and public security. He has published (or co-published) more than 200 papers in refereed international journals and holds several patents.
